title: Cosmic Collision: Woman Hit by Space Rock

title: Rainbow Breaks Records in Taiwan
story: Rainbows usually vanish in minutes, but in 2017, one decided to stick around. On November 30, people in Taipei, Taiwan, gazed in wonder at a rainbow that stretched across the sky for nearly nine hours! This colorful arc appeared over the Yangmingshan mountains, delighting onlookers from morning until late afternoon. Scientists confirmed it as the longest-lasting rainbow ever recorded, proving that sometimes nature likes to put on an extra-special show.

title: Ancient Amazon Art Amazes Archaeologists
story: Explorers in the Colombian Amazon made a jaw-dropping discovery in 2020. Hidden in the Serranía La Lindosa region, they found thousands of rock paintings created by ancient artists. These colorful masterpieces, dating back nearly 12,000 years, show animals from the Ice Age that no longer exist today. Imagine seeing giant sloths and mastodons come to life on rocky canvases! This incredible find on November 30 gives us a window into a long-lost world.

title: Golden Face from the Past
story: Buried for thousands of years, a stunning golden mask waited to be found. In 1876, archaeologist Heinrich Schliemann unearthed this treasure at Mycenae, Greece. The mask, with its piercing eyes and regal beard, became known as the Mask of Agamemnon. People were amazed by its craftsmanship, calling it the Mona Lisa of prehistory. On November 30, this gleaming face from ancient times saw daylight again, connecting us to a long-lost world.

story: Batter up! In 1887, softball took an exciting turn. Players swapped sunny fields for cozy indoor spaces on November 30. Chicago hosted the very first indoor softball game. This clever twist let fans enjoy their favorite sport even when chilly winds blew outside. It was a home run idea that changed how we play ball!
activity_question: Where was the first indoor softball game played?

title: Picture Perfect: Crossing the Atlantic by Radio
story: Snap! Click! In 1924, a groundbreaking moment in communication occurred. The first photo facsimile zipped across the Atlantic Ocean by radio waves. This amazing feat connected London to New York City, spanning thousands of miles. On November 30, people marveled as an image traveled from one continent to another, paving the way for faster global information sharing.
activity_question: How did the first photo facsimile travel across the Atlantic?
follow_up_question: Can you think of ways we share photos instantly today?

title: Chicago's Underwater Highway Adventure
story: Imagine driving through a tunnel under a river! In 1866, Chicago started building America's first underwater highway tunnel. Workers dug deep beneath the Chicago River, creating a secret passage for horses and carriages. This amazing project showed how cities could solve traffic problems in clever ways. On November 30, construction began on this groundbreaking tunnel.
activity_question: What was special about the tunnel Chicago started building in 1866?
activity_choices: It was America's first underwater highway tunnel
It was the longest tunnel in the world
It was made entirely of glass
It connected Chicago to New York
activity_answer: It was America's first underwater highway tunnel
follow_up_question: Why do you think building an underwater tunnel was a clever solution for Chicago?
